text:
I find indigo deeply underrated. It occupies this fascinating middle ground between blue and purple that most people overlook, often conflating it with either one or the other. It's the color of twilight—that specific moment when the sky isn't quite night yet but has abandoned the day.

I associate it with introspection and mystery, but not in a dark or foreboding way. There's something contemplative about it, almost meditative. It's the color you might find in old denim that's been worn soft, in certain twilight photographs taken just at the edge of dusk, or in certain deep ocean waters. It has weight and substance to it without being heavy.

What strikes me about indigo is that it seems to exist in people's peripheral awareness. When you ask someone their favorite color, they rarely say indigo, even though it appears everywhere in nature and culture. There's a quietness to it that makes it easy to overlook in favor of colors that demand more attention. Yet that restraint is part of what makes it compelling—it suggests depth rather than announcing it.
